Manx: Tra ta yn derrey vought cooney lesh bought elley, ta Jee hene garaghtee
English: ‘When one poor man relieves another, God himself rejoices at it;” (or, as it is in the original) ‘he laughs outright.”
Notes: [A Tour Through the Isle of Man, D. Robertosn. Pub. E. Hodson, London. 1794.

Manx: Ny freggyr ommydan cordail rish e ommijys, er aaggle dy bee oo hene casley rishyn.
English: Do not answer a fool according to his stupidity for fear that you yourself will be like him.

Manx: Jeeagh shiu cre ta me [mee] er gheddyn voish Aspick Murrey ... cha nel eh er yarrood me [mee].
English: See what I have received from Bishop Murray ... he has not forgotten me.
‘a snuff he manufactured by himself from tobacco leaves dried by the fire, which he carried in an old fashioned horn, resembling a powder horn’.

Manx: “Dy jean foast yn billey-olive failleil, as nagh jean ny magheryn gymmyrkey beaghey; dy bee yn chioltane giarit jeh veih’n woaillee, as nagh bee dew ayns ny bwaneyn.’
English: “the labour of the olive shall fail, and the fields shall yield no meat; the flock shall be cut off from the fold, and there shall be no herd in the stalls:” [(Habakkuk 3:17)

Manx: Augh! as augh! my griah Ballaragh, [Augh! as augh! My ghriah Ballaragh,]
Vallaragh, my chree, cha vaik ym oo arragh; [Vallaragh, my chree, cha vaikym oo arragh;]
English: Ballaragh of my heart I shall never see thee more;

Manx: Ta mee my hassoo, as my ghriem rish y volley,— [Ta mee my hassoo, as my ghreeym rish y voalley,]
English: Here I stand with my back to the wall,

Manx: Cummal seoise kiole gys cloan ny moadea! [Cummal seose kiuall gys cloan ny moddee!]
English: Keeping up music to the children of dogs.

Manx: “Agh nee my Yee ooilley nyn veme y yannoo magh cordail rish e verchys ayns gloyr, liorish Creest Yeesey.”
English: “But my God will satisfy all our needs according to the his wealth in glory, by Jesus Christ.”

Manx: As dooyrt y Chiarn Jee, Cur-my.ner ta’n dooinney myryien er jeet dy ve myr unnane-jinyn, dy hoiggal mie as sie: as nish son nagh der eh magh e laue, as dy gow eh myrgeddin jeh billey’n vea, as gee jeh, as ve dy bragh beayn;
English: And the Lord God said, Behold the man is become as one of us, to know good and evil: and now, lest he put forth his hand, and take also of the tree of life, and eat, and live for ever:

Manx: Shen-y-fa hug y Chiarn Jee magh eh ass garey Eden dy obbraghey yn thalloo, veih v’eh er ny ghoaill.
English: Therefore the Lord God sent him forth from the garden of Eden, to till the ground from whence he was taken.
The mother tongue of the Isle of Man
... the proper Manks for earthquake, and not “craa-thallooin”...

Notes: [Mona Miscellany; Edited by W. Harrison: Manx Society Vol. XVI; 1869]
[Mona Miscellany; Edited by W. Harrison: Manx Society Vol. XVI; 1869]

Manx: Bea-veayn da’n kiannoort vie ain Hal Brougham Loch
English: Long-life to our good governor Hal Brougham Loch

Manx:
Eh nagh dug scammylt rieau, dy ghoaill 
Veih naboo e ghoo mie ;
As nagh dug cleaysh da’n tutlar broghe,
Ta shooyl veih thie dy thie.
English:
He that never gave harsh words to take 
the good name from a neighbour, 
and never gave ear to the filthy gossip 
that walks from house to house.

Manx: Bee-jee twooaigh jeh mraane as moddee.
Agh te er daghyrt eh cordail rish y chenn raa firrinagh. Ta’n moddey er jyndaa reesht gys y skeeah echey hene; as y vuc va nieet, gys yn ymmylt eck sy laagh.
English: But it has happened in accordance with the truthful old saying. The dog has returned to his own vomit; and the pig that was washed, to her rolling in the muck.

Manx: Aght erbee t’eh ceaut nee eh shassoo
English: Whichever way it is thrown it will stand

Manx: Cha jean oo vaggyr mee, as cha jean mee vaggyr ort.
English: (If) You don’t threaten me, then I won’t threaten you.
A spotted heifer on a lead.

Manx: Cre raad dys Thie-Saggyrt jeh Kirk Andreays
Notes: What’s the way to the vicarage of  Kirk Andreas

Manx: Cha row ee rieau ny share.
As signal dys y laue chaire.
Notes: [likely meaning; signing for the side opposing the lord in a dispute around the Act of Settlement]

English: John, son of Patrick (of) the barn

Manx: f ee saaue ays y laagh
graye yee bee shee ooin esh [graih yee bee shee ayns eisht]
English: (for) God's sake there will be peace then.

Notes: [Juan y Jagged Keir gives this as the nickname of a certain ‘tailor, ... Foreman of a Cabbage and Goose Society, Chairman of a Watch Club’. ‘Chrosh’ means cross, ‘Darrig’, here is likely [darragh] ‘oak’. ‘Cross of Oak’.]

English: Billy of the Pictures / Images / Statues.

Manx: “Bock-bane-na-Chanie doll shish an beyr” [“Bock bane ny [??] goll sheese yn bayr”]
English: “The white gelding of the [??] going down the road”

Manx: “As dooyrt eh daue, lhig shiu dy gholl shaghey yn scrieu choontee, as scrieu kiare feed thousane punt son lhiastynys Phurt Yiarn.”
English: “And he said to them, neglect the budget, and write (issue) eighty thousand pounds for Port Erin’s debt.”

Notes: [Broderick, G. says this was a common address used by native speakers and fluent L2 speakers of Late Spoken Manx.]
[‘Nan-A-Creggan’s Christmas Eve’ by Juan Lewin; M.H., 07/01/1880]

English: Jonny of the Lapping up / Folding

Manx: Vieh slieau yn clagen baney [Veih slieau ny claghyn baney]
English: From the the Mountain of the White Stones

Manx: Shoh nane oss [as], shoh nane ellah [elley]
English: Here’s one, and here’s another [‘the way Jemmy Dach used to count herrings’]
Little Tom the Weaver / Tom the Weaver Jr.
Palmaria Palmata, Dulse [a type of seaweed]
‘Welcome to the noble chairman’ [‘The genial welcome of the chariman’]

Manx: Dy choyrt dwoaie; dwoiagh; feoh da.
Dy hannaghtyn; dy uirraghtyn syn un voayl
Cha nee tra ta’n keyrrey gee yn ouw t’eh çheet r’ee.
English: It is not when the sheep eats the marsh penny wort that it appears to her.

Manx: Fow aarloo dy gholl quail dy yee.
English: Be ready to meet thy god
Notes: [Chapter IV, ‘Manx Recollections’, Memorials of Eleanor Elliott, by K. A. Forrest, James Nisbett & Co., Douglas, 1894.]

English: In the earlier part of this century the herrings were sold by the “cran,” but so much imposition was practised that it had to be discontinued. By Act of Tynwald in 1817 (see Statutes. vol. I. pp. 397-8) the “cran” contained 42 gallons.
The fisherman in command of the fleet..  [His duties were especially to guide them as to time for shooting and hauling their nets. Shooting the nets before sunset was forbidden by law.]
The Hill of the Rising Day
(the) earnest, an advance payment as a pledge for the remainder

[‘— As they neared the port the “bimming yawls,” or buyers, boarded them, and, after they had made their bargain, they handed the fishermen a shilling or other coin, called the eearlys, or “earnest” and passed round a bottle of rum, to which every one on board helped himself out of a horn measure. The bargain was then completed.’
[This name is said to have originated from that of] a large fishing smack, so-called, which was built about the year 1750.]
Notes: [The Biblical name ‘Dagon’ is a Philistine god of fishing.  Boat of this name is said to have run aground at ‘Gob y Dagon’, now known as Go-y-Deigan. [M.H., 01/01/1896]

English: an activity done ‘against doors’ on Hollantide Fair night (thumping doors with vegetables).
Term of endearment, eg; darling, love, honey, dear, etc.

‘Hairy stockings, or shaggy legs’ ... ‘an owl fairy that used to haunt the magher runt [round field] near St. Rinyans ... near Croshby [Crosby] — (‘bannished from Fairyland because he missed attending the) reholleys vooar, or great festival of the Harvest Moon.

Notes: Flyer for a bring-and-buy sale in St Matthew’s New School [MNH F71/MAT 35816] 30th & 31st October 1912.]

Manx: Fill Beg na Merlin [Phil Beg ny Murlin]
Fill Beg na -Murlin [Phil Beg ny Murlin]
Filly beg na Claughin [Phillee Beg ny Claghyn]
English: Little Philly / Philly Junior (of) the stones (or, colloquially;  testicles)
Frenchman [here used as a nickname for a wine dealer]

Notes: [Here found in: ‘Got up on their friggans’  [got annoyed / took offence][M.H.,12/011898]

Manx: Fuirree yn mwaagh rish e heshey !
English: The hare will wait for his mate (partner) !
George, son of Tom, son of Jack

Manx: Gow gee [Gow-jee] tastey, ta’n trass lhaih Gailckagh dy ghoaill toshaight ayns Halley C. Myleworrey ec shaight er y chlagh syn astyr.
English: Take note, the third Manx reading to begin in C. Morrison’s Hall at seven o’clock in the evening.

Manx: graye yee bee shee ooin esh
English: For God’s sake will we have peace then?

Notes: Juan-yn-Jagged Keir at the North: M.H.,11/10/1876;

English: a kind of reddish moss which grows plentifully on the rocks by the sea

Manx: “Dy gooidsave lhiat dy choyrt as dy reayll gys yn ymmyd ain messyn dooie y thallooin, (as dy chur er ash as dy hannaghtyn dooin bannaghtyn ny marrey), myr shen ayns nyn imbagh cooie dy vod mayd yn soylley oc y gheddyn,”
English: “That it may please Thee to bless and preserve to our use the kindly fruits of the earth, (and to restore and continue unto us the blessings of the sea), so that in due time we may enjoy them,”
Notes: [‘Bishop Wilson's interpolation (still used every Sunday in most of the Manx churches)’] [M.S.,07/09/1889]

Manx: Haul! Ta’n lhaa [laa] brishey harrish y cronk.
English: Haul! The day breaks over the hill.
Notes: (Called out to signal daybreak — time to haul in the nets). [M.H.,01/011896]

Manx: Hig e chiarail márish, slioar da’n laa t’ayn e heaghyn hene.[Hig e chiarail marish; s’lioar da’n laa t’ayn e heaghyn hene.]
English: His plan will come together, his own sorrows are enough for today.

network, section of a net [Each jeebin was fifty-two meshes deep, and seventeen yards long. Sixteen of them formed a net, i.e., four in length and four in depth. The nets were made of flas or hemp, which was span at home during the long winter nights. The sixteen jeebins that formed the net were joined together in much the same fashion as canvas in a sail. If one jeebin got worn out, or failed, it was taken out and another put in its place. If the mesh shrank and became too small it was condemned. If an English shilling passed freely between knot and knot the mesh was considered sufficient.]
Jimmy of the Nappin (either ‘of the turnip’ or ‘of the Nappin farm’.

Manx: Juan Clucaish, Lunning-veg, as Mrs Coole
English: John Clucas, Little London, and Mrs Coole
(the) big bridge (Ramsey’s stone bridge, across the Sulby)

Manx: thie oast na mullyr [Thie oast ny Mwyllar]
English: The Miller’s Inn (The Royal George Hotel, now known as Barbery Coast North).
Notes: [Owned by the Monk family who also owned the Ramsey’s windmill]

English: Little Jurby of the scoundrels / scabs.
Notes: [This is verse from a song titled ‘Arrane y Whee Hoa’ about the Act of Settlement, the exact meaning of which is obscure, although ‘y Vuinneel Veg’ seems to be a nickname for Bishop Wilson.] [M.H.,17/10/1877]

Manx: Cha row ee rieau ny share
As signal dys y laue chaire
English: And signing to the right hand(side)

Manx: kys ne vel sheen ghetthen Dhaney gorish a shaw
English: how are we not getting men like him?

Manx: [Kys nagh vel shin geddyn deiney gollrish eh shoh]:

English: With her, with her, with her (shouted together when launching boats).
Notes: [ ‘towards her’ may be a better translation?] [M.H.,01/011896]

Manx: Litcheragh goll dy lhie litcheragh dy irree, as litcheragh dy gholl dys y cheeill jedoonee
English: Sluggish to go to bed, sluggish to rise, and sluggish to go the church on Sunday.
brown wool from the loghtan sheep

Manx: graye yee bee shee ooin esh [graih Yee, bee shee ayn eisht]
English: For God’s sake, there’ll be peace then
‘Great Mam’ [ie: Queen Victoria, head of the Church of England]

Manx: Mannagh gyn scoghey murn scrieuym reesht. (Mannagh jean scughey moyrn screeuym reesht).
English: If (it) doesn’t remove pride [humble him] I’ll write again.

Manx:
Moar yn Kella,
Preaçheil son mooarane blein,
Cha row fys echey dys myleeaney,
Dy row yn jouyl ayns jough as feeyn.
English:
Moore of the Kella,
Preaching for many years,
He didn’t know until this year,
That the devil was in beer and wine.

Notes: [They (the fishermen) sold their herrings by the mease of five hundred, there being six score and four fish to the hundred. They counted the herrings as follows: two men got a basket between them, when they commenced with unnane, jees, three, i.e., one, two, three, warp, and so on alternately to daeed, or forty, when there was added another warp and a single herring, which was called “tally” thus making 121 fish. The skipper carefully marked each “tally” by making a notch with his knife on a stick. Every fifth notch crossed the other four, and this made a mease.] [M.H.,01/011896]

Manx: My dooinney veen ta Pashance ayd son kiare bleiney jeig, as cha nel shiu veg share foast.
English: My dear man, you have (had) ‘Patience’ for fourteen years, and you are no better yet.

Manx: “My vac, ny soie beg jeh smaght y Chiarn, chamoo lhig dty chree voïd tra t’eh coyrt seaghyn dty raad. Son ta’n Chiarn smaghtaghey eshyn shynney lesh; as kerraghey dy chooilley vac t’eh goaill-rish.”—*
English: “My son, do not despise the chastisement of the Lord, neither lose your heart when he send distress your way. For the Lord chastises he he most loves; and punishes every son he acknowledges.”

Manx: Myr dooyrt y Vuggane mooar, ny cur eïe orryms as cha derryms eie ort!
English: As the big buggane said; don’t meddle with me and and I won’t meddle with you!

Manx:
Nee goll thie t’ou? 
Cha nee.
English:
Is it going home you are? / Are you going home?
No.
Is it going home thou art? [‘wast’ expected here]

Notes: [About 1850 a number of Cornish fishermen came to Peel with their boats, and, as Nicholas, or Nickey, was a common name among them, it was applied to their rig, which was then very generally adopted by the Manx boats, which also took the name of “Nickeys.”] [M.H.,01/011896]

Manx: Ny Poose eirey-inneen ny slooid ny ta’n ayr eck er ny ve croghit
English: ‘navar marry an hearess onless her father wes hung’
Oie’l Verree (Christmas Eve singing event in a church)

Manx: O Juan, Juan, gow chrael jeed hene; [O Juan, Juan, gow kiarail jeed hene]
English: O Juan, Juan, take care of yourself;

Manx: Ollick vie chennal (sic) as blein feer vie.
English: A good cheery Christmas and good new year.
[The herb in question is the] marsh penny wort, [known in the Island as the ouw, pronounced ow.]
[Nickname for] ‘The Isle of Man Steam Packet Company”.

Notes: [So much of the net as extended from one float to another. There were five “pairs” to each net. [M.H.,01/011896]

Manx: Palchys as maynrys gys Ellan Vannin.
English: Plenty and happiness to the Isle of Man.

Manx: Poosey dy moghey, as poosey dy mennick.

Notes: [Advert for W. M. Corlett’s North-star Boot and Shoe Depot, 21 West Quay.

English: Were you proving there, boy. [Greeting between fishermen of different boats looking for fish.]
[a perjorative, likely a variant of ‘riftan’] a worthless fellow.
the Manx term applied to the English race

Manx: Shee dy Vea Mannin —Shee Yee dy row Meriu
English: Welcome (to) the Isle of Man — may God’s Peace be with you [inscription on Mr Noble’s house for a royal visit]

Manx: Shee Dy Vea gys Glion Laxey

English: the boat’s supper [— This was held about Christmas time, very often on old St Stephen’s night (January 7th), or old New Year’s night (January 13th). On this occasion there was provided an abundance of pies and puddings, and plenty of rum and jough. Each member of the crew could bring his wife or sweetheart, and so largely did they indulge that these suppers have been known to cost from five to ten pounds.]
This, a pup of the first litter

Notes: [In Anglo-Manx Dialect ‘Law! ’, or ‘Lor!’’, [Loayr], is used as an intensifier as [abair] is in Scottish Gaelic (although, it is commonly taken to be a contraction of ‘Lord’).

English: drink got on credit (at the start of the fishing season)

Manx: Slaynt dy row lhiat Ayr Ullick

English: Gaelic war-cry which ever rang as precious music in the ears of the warlike Stanley—
[a seat with a back, equivalent to a chair
a straw rope made on the thumb.

Manx: Ta aggle y chiarn toshiaght vashtee.
English: Fear of the lord is the beginning of baptism.

Manx: Ta airh er ny cushaghyn ayns shoh.
English: There's gold on the cushags here. (Manxmen used to say whenever anything was said against the Island)

Manx: Ta Albinagh er e ockle as astan er e amman, daa red feer doillee dy chummal.
English: A Scotsman on (by) his word and an eel on (by) his tail, two things that are very difficult to hold.

Manx: Ta creenaght ny share na rubieyn.
Ta drogh hammag ny share na magher foshlit
English: A bad bush in a gap is better than an open field

Manx: Ta keeayll ommidjys ny slooid na t’ee ec dooinney creeney dy reayll.
English: Wit is folly unless it be in the possession of a wise man.

Manx: Ta mee credjal nagh vod slane Elian Vannin sauail yn guilley shoh veih cheet dy ve ommydan.
English: I believe the whole Isle of Man cannot save this boy from being a fool.
The Fair is now under command

Manx: Ta shin Di-jerroo jercal dy be yn calloo shoh jeh vondeish erskyn towse mooar da’n Ellan ain
English: We sincerely expect [hope] that this pier will be of immeasurably positive benefit to our Island.
Notes: [THE NEW LANDlNG PIER  (Messrs Cowle): M.H., , July 03, 1872]

Manx: “Ta shin dy-jarrow jerkal dy bee yn Calloo shoh jeh vondeish erskyn towse mooar da’n Elian ain.”
English: “We sincerely hope that this Pier will be of immeasurably great benefit to our Island.”
Notes: [COMPLETION OF THE BATTERY PIER. (The Isle of Man Harbour Commissioners): M.H., , September 03, 1879]

Manx: Ta shin Jarroo Jercal dy be Yn Calloo shoh jeh Vondeish erskyn Towse Mooar da'n Elian. [Ta shin dy jarroo jerkal dy bee yn calloo shoh jeh vondeish erskyn towse mooar da’n Ellan].
English: We indeed expect that this pier will be of immeasurably great benefit to the Island.

Manx: Ta ynsagh coamrey stoamey yn dooinney berchagh, as t’eh berchys yn dooinney boght.
English: Learning is the fine clothing of the rich man, and it is the wealth of the poor man.

Manx: The jarroo thee Cairt; The jarroo thee Cairt
English: Indeed, she’s right; Indeed, she’s right.

Manx: [Dy jarroo, t’ee kiart; Dy jarroo, t’ee kiart]
Thie oast na Mullyr [Thie Ooast ny Mwyllar]
Tra hig yn laa, hig yn coyrle lesh.
English: When the day comes, the counsel will come with it.

Manx: Vel Pagh er-bee gol gys Lhie ayns Keill-e-Pheric ? ... Skeerey-Pheric, Skeerey Marooney, Skeerey-Conaghan, Skeerey-Braddan, Skeery-Steldane , Skeerey-Malew ... Skeerey-Chreest-Rushen. English: “Is there any person going to Law in St Patrick’s Church? ... Parish of Patrick, Marown, Onchan, Braddan, St. Anne (Santon), Malew, ... Kirk Christ Rushen ?”
A vessel with two masts rigged with fore and aft sails

Manx: Vr Harry Callister, Skeerey Pherick ... Arrane ny Ferrishyn (song of the fairies).
English: Mr Harry Callister, Patrick Parish ... Song of the Fairies.
[perjorative aimed at ‘Watchman’ – a writer alleged to have slandered the ‘Peel Good Templars’].
